profile.name: Prentiss
profile.title: Frontier Operator
profile.headline: Building AI-native operating systems and coordination layers

projects.name: GetIT
projects.system_design: Video-first transactional local services marketplace. Core documented MVP includes h-app editing, high-quality video playback, real-time notifications, payments, brief recommendations, AI-based tagging/transcription. Longer-term direction: geo-boosting, video insight, contextual personalization, voice, offline-on-device, and ambient layers.

projects.name: Fast Food
projects.system_design: AI-native family meal-planning and dinner-decision operating system. Local-first intelligence with biometric learning stored in Supabase. Context-aware inputs including living geography. NaiveExpo path established for navigation tooling. Expansion UX intended to stay short by default.

projects.name: Anansi
projects.short_desc: AI-native platform converting lessons into playable educational games
projects.system_design: AI-native platform that tunes lessons into playable indie-grade educational games. Concept is lesson-to-game generation positioned between serious edtech and fun-but-shallow learning products. Targets three audiences simultaneously: kids (engagement), teachers (results + evidence), parents (simple, engaging experience).

projects.name: North Star
projects.short_desc: Knowledge graph constellation rendering founder capability
projects.system_design: Knowledge graph / constellation-style product centered on a single founder profile. Three core surfaces: Constellation Canvas (force-directed graph visualization), Node Expansion Panel (drill-down detail), Ask-the-Graph interface (semantic query layer). Graph engine renders people/projects/skills/decisions/work with clustering, node expansion, and graph querying as core interaction patterns. Initial scope: Single Profile v0 with stable rendering, performance budgets, zoom levels, and clarity acceptance criteria.

nodes.title: Marketplace Trust & Recommendation Transparency
nodes.description: Marketplace trust and recommendation transparency matter. Expansion into ambient platform materiality requires explicitly noting ethical issues around material requirements.

nodes.title: Real-Time Notifications Must Scale
nodes.description: Real-time notifications in marketplace context require reliable infrastructure and user experience at scale. Implicit in MVP design.

nodes.title: Ambient Platform Materiality Raises Ethical Questions
nodes.description: Ethical issues around material requirements and ambient platform behavior are explicitly noted as a separate constraint from marketplace trust.

nodes.title: Invite-Only Controlled Launch Strategy
nodes.description: Launch first with invite-only controlled access. Phased expansion with trust-building as part of GTM logic, rather than attempting full marketplace launch in one month.

nodes.title: Prioritize Video-First Interaction
nodes.description: Make video the primary medium for service discovery, transactions, and user interaction. Core architectural choice.

nodes.title: MVP Scope: H-app, Playback, Notifications, Payments, Recommendations
nodes.description: Define minimal viable product to include h-app editing, high-quality video playback, real-time notifications, payments integration, and AI-powered recommendations. Deliberately bounded scope.

nodes.title: Use AI-Based Tagging and Transcription
nodes.description: Leverage AI models for automatic metadata generation (tagging, transcription) to reduce editorial overhead and improve searchability.

nodes.title: Watch-Through Rate (Primary KPI)
nodes.description: Watch-through rates on GetIT, clicks, and ASR buffer response. MVP capabilities are descriptive rather than prescriptive; testing and UX iteration ongoing.

nodes.title: No Formal Business Metrics Documented
nodes.description: GetIT lacks formal business metrics (GMV, CAC, retention, vendor density). Focus has been on MVP capabilities and testing, not yet on business model measurement.

nodes.title: MVP Capabilities Established and Documented
nodes.description: Sufficient progress on MVP capabilities for founder networking, iteration, and booster testing. Product philosophy and UX direction coherent.

nodes.title: Must Remain Simple, Calm, Mature
nodes.description: Product must remain simple, calm, mature, and low-friction for mentally-consumed users. Local-first is an architectural constraint. Weather integration and accessibility treated as first-pass areas.

nodes.title: Accessibility Must Be First-Pass
nodes.description: Accessibility is treated as a first-pass design constraint, not an afterthought. Affects interaction design and scope decisions.

nodes.title: Local-First Architecture (Over Cloud-First)
nodes.description: Chose local-first over cloud-first design. Data stored in Supabase with context-aware inputs. Supports offline capability and user agency over remote synchronization.

nodes.title: Position as AI-Native Meal-Planning Operating System
nodes.description: Frame Fast Food as an AI-native family meal-planning and dinner-decision operating system, not just a recipe app. OS framing implies systematic, extensible approach.

nodes.title: Integrate Biometric Learning for Personalization
nodes.description: Use biometric learning (stored in Supabase) to personalize meal and dinner suggestions. Context-aware inputs include geography and user preferences.

nodes.title: NaiveExpo Chosen for Navigation
nodes.description: NaiveExpo path established for navigation tooling. One-line decision explanation. Baseline UX intent is to stay short by default during expansion.

nodes.title: Keep Expansion UX Short by Default
nodes.description: As Fast Food expands features, maintain the constraint that new UX paths stay short. Do not accumulate complexity over time.

nodes.title: Strongly Developed Product Philosophy
nodes.description: Fast Food has developed a strong product philosophy and interaction design approach. Initial interaction design documented and coherent. Expansion UX intent is clear (stay short).

nodes.title: Expansion UX Approach Designed to Preserve Simplicity
nodes.description: Through constraint navigation, developed a clear expansion UX strategy that keeps complexity low and interaction paths short as Fast Food grows.

nodes.title: Biometric Learning Path Established
nodes.description: Biometric learning architecture designed and integrated with Supabase storage. Feature realization demonstrates AI-product integration at scale.

nodes.title: Must Satisfy Three Audiences Simultaneously
nodes.description: Product must satisfy three distinct audiences at once: kids (need engagement), teachers (need results and evidence), parents (need simplicity and engagement). Cannot optimize for one at expense of others.

nodes.title: AI-Native Lesson-to-Game Generation as Wedge
nodes.description: Positioning Anansi as AI-native platform with lesson-to-game generation as the core wedge. Concept positioned between serious edtech and fun-but-shallow learning products. Rather than generic quiz or software approach.

nodes.title: Core Algorithm: Lesson-to-Game Transformation
nodes.description: Build core capability to transform educational lessons into playable, engaging indie-grade games. This is the primary differentiation vs. traditional edtech.

nodes.title: Strong Opportunity Framing & Strategic Story
nodes.description: Anansi has developed strong opportunity framing and strategic narrative. Concept occupies clear position in edtech spectrum. Tech approach aligned with market timing and audience needs.

nodes.title: Lesson-to-Game Generation Model Established
nodes.description: Core lesson-to-game transformation model conceptually established and positioned as the market differentiator. Validates AI-native approach to educational game design.

nodes.title: Start with Single Profile v0 (Not Multi-User)
nodes.description: Explicit decision to begin with single founder profile rendering, not multi-user network. Scope locked from source. Necessary for proof of concept before expanding to broader universe.

nodes.title: Three Core Surfaces: Canvas, Panel, Ask-the-Graph
nodes.description: Define exactly three primary UX surfaces: Constellation Canvas (graph visualization), Node Expansion Panel (detail view), Ask-the-Graph interface (query layer). Bounded scope prevents feature creep.

nodes.title: Force-Directed Layout with Stored Positions
nodes.description: Use force-directed algorithm (d3-force) to compute initial node positions. Store positions in database for stability and repeatability. No runtime recomputation unless content changes.

nodes.title: Define Hard Performance Targets
nodes.description: Lock explicit performance budgets: LCP <2.0s, 55-60 FPS target, <50ms hover latency, <150ms click-to-panel. No vague 'fast enough' — measure against M1 MacBook baseline.

nodes.title: Enable Bloom Post-Processing
nodes.description: Use Three.js bloom effect for visual clarity and glow. Settings: strength 0.5, radius 0.8, threshold 0.8. Improves visual feedback without overwhelming the interface.

nodes.title: Accessibility: Reduced Motion Support Required
nodes.description: Detect and respect prefers-reduced-motion media query. Disable all animations (instant transitions instead) for users with motion sensitivity.

nodes.title: Must Feel Immersive & Beautiful Without Visual Heaviness
nodes.description: Interface must feel immersive and beautiful without becoming visually heavy or cluttered. Current phase focuses on maintaining clarity. Document spec direction for single profile v0. Interface integrity depends on clear information hierarchy and avoiding over-rendering.

nodes.title: Desktop-First Rendering; Mobile Deferred
nodes.description: Optimize rendering for desktop viewports first. Mobile support deferred to Phase 2+. Initial v0 targets laptop/desktop screens (1280px+).

nodes.title: Three Core Surfaces Must Maintain Interaction Integrity
nodes.description: Constellation Canvas, Node Expansion Panel, and Ask-the-Graph interface must work flawlessly together. Navigation between surfaces must be seamless and predictable.

nodes.title: Graph Model Mistakes & UX Dead Ends (Early)
nodes.description: One clear failure already surfaced: drifted into premature graph-model mistakes and UX dead ends. Recovered by restructuring scope to single profile v0 with explicit performance budgets and clarity criteria. Learning: lock content and rendering spec before code.

nodes.title: Product Concept Model & Spec Direction Documented
nodes.description: North Star has already produced meaningful outputs including product concept model, specification direction, and reference implementation targets. Constellation Canvas, Node Expansion Panel, and Ask-the-Graph interface models established. Three-screen interface architecture clear.

nodes.title: Rendering Specification Locked
nodes.description: Complete rendering spec with hard values: node sizes, colors, camera presets, bloom settings, LOD breakpoints, performance budgets. Ready for Phase 2 implementation.

nodes.title: API Contract Specified (OpenAPI 3.0)
nodes.description: Four endpoints specified in OpenAPI 3.0: GET /graph/profile/:id, GET /graph/node/:id, POST /graph/query, POST /graph/layout/recompute. Ready for backend implementation.

nodes.title: Marketplace System Design
nodes.description: Demonstrated expertise in designing transactional marketplaces with trust, transparency, and recommendation systems. Applied across GetIT (video services) and broader platform thinking.

nodes.title: Local-First Architecture & Offline Capability
nodes.description: Deep expertise in designing systems that prioritize local data storage, offline-first operation, and user agency. Applied in Fast Food and ambient layer thinking for GetIT.

nodes.title: AI-Native Product Integration
nodes.description: Expertise in integrating AI models into consumer-facing products. Demonstrated in GetIT (AI tagging, transcription), Fast Food (meal planning), Anansi (lesson-to-game generation). Comfortable with constraints and tradeoffs of language models in production.

nodes.title: Operating Under Hard Constraints
nodes.description: Demonstrated ability to define and operate within hard constraints (marketplace trust, simplicity, three-audience design, single profile scope). Uses constraints as design force, not obstacle. Explicitly notes ethical and material constraints.

nodes.title: Knowledge Graph & Constellation Design
nodes.description: Expertise in designing knowledge graph systems that render capability and decision-making patterns visually. Clear thinking on visualization, query semantics, and progressive disclosure. North Star demonstrates mature understanding of graph-as-interface.

nodes.title: Video-Product User Experience
nodes.description: Expertise in designing products centered on video interaction, playback quality, and video-first marketplaces. GetIT demonstrates deep understanding of video UX, transcription, and video-driven transactions.

nodes.title: AI-Native Operating System Design
nodes.description: Pattern across all three projects: GetIT as 'ambient OS', Fast Food as 'meal-planning OS', Anansi as 'game OS'. Demonstrates thinking about software as operating systems (extensible, ambient, context-aware) rather than individual apps.

nodes.title: Real-Time Interaction & Notification Systems
nodes.description: Expertise in designing real-time systems with <50ms latency targets. Demonstrated in GetIT (real-time notifications) and North Star (performance budgets <50ms hover, <150ms click-to-panel).

nodes.title: Accessibility-First UX Design
nodes.description: Deep commitment to accessibility as a first-pass constraint (not afterthought). Demonstrated in Fast Food (accessibility first-pass area) and North Star (reduced-motion support required).

nodes.title: Educational Game & Learning Product Design
nodes.description: Expertise in designing educational products that engage multiple stakeholders (students, educators, parents). Anansi demonstrates deep understanding of lesson-to-game transformation and audience-appropriate game design.

nodes.title: Maintaining Simplicity as Design Discipline
nodes.description: Rare ability to enforce simplicity constraints across product evolution. Fast Food demonstrates this through 'simple, calm, mature' constraint enforced across multiple decisions (local-first, accessibility, expansion UX short).

nodes.title: Long-Horizon Architecture Planning
nodes.description: All projects mention future vision and ambient layers. Demonstrates ability to design for long-term extensibility (ambient layers, expansion, universe scaling) while shipping focused v0.

Force-directed layout decision demonstrates graph design expertise
Performance budget targets require real-time systems expertise

Desktop-first constraint reinforces single-profile v0 scope decision
Early graph model failures drove decision to lock single profile scope and specs
Clarity discipline has produced documented concept model

Both projects demonstrate density-first marketplace and AI-native operating system thinking
Both must satisfy multiple audiences simultaneously (families, kids/teachers/parents)
Both position as OS-style products with ambient/future vision (ambient layers, game OS)
All projects demonstrate consistent constraint-first design philosophy

Anansi navigates three-audience constraint to design differentiated positioning
North Star navigates clarity, desktop-first, and single-profile constraints

graph_metadata.density_assessment: CREDIBLE — Four projects with detailed decisions, constraints, failures, outcomes, and demonstrated skills. Graph now shows clear structure: project clusters connected by shared patterns, skills bridge projects, long-horizon thinking visible across all. Enough content to render meaningful constellation with multiple discovery paths.

gaps_acknowledged.getit: No formal business metrics documented (GMV, CAC, retention, vendor density)
gaps_acknowledged.fastfood: No formal metrics established; user outcome data missing
gaps_acknowledged.anansi: No pilot data, classroom results, or quantified learning efficacy
gaps_acknowledged.general: All projects: user testimonials, validation stories, scale metrics not documented
